sections.content:

It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The board has three regions: (1) top-left shows a target pattern with white block above purple block, (2) top-right has two selector buttons (white and purple) with an indicator bar, (3) main area has a purple block above a white block (inverted from target). The goal appears to be matching the target pattern.
- Plan: Let me examine the selector button positions more carefully and try clicking the frame itself. Also try a fresh sequence: white selector → SPACE → purple selector → SPACE.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 20 of about 55 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see the layout clearly from the ascii:
- Row 2: Two button frames at cols 35-39 (white) and cols 41-45 (purple)
- Row 3-5: Button contents: white at cols 36-38, purple at cols 42-44
- Row 7: Indicator bar at cols 41-45 (under purple button)
- Target panel: white block at rows 3-7, cols 3-12; purple block at rows 8-12, cols 3-12
The indicator is at [[7, 41], [7, 45]] which is under the PURPLE button (cols 41-45). This makes sense since the framed block is purple.
Now let me think about what the game might want. The target shows white-above-purple. The current state has:
- Framed block: purple (7×12)
- Unframed: purple top (5×10) + white bottom (5×10)
The target is white(5×10) above purple(5×10). The unframed block when split is purple-above-white, which is the REVERSE of the target.
What if the game wants me to swap the order? What if I need to somehow make the white part be on top and purple on bottom?
Wait, what if the framed block IS the "top" part and the unframed block IS the "bottom" part? The target is white-above-purple. So:
- Framed block (top) should be white
- Unframed block (bottom) should be purple
Currently:
- Framed: purple (needs to be white)
- Unframed: purple top + white bottom (needs to be all purple)
To make the unframed block all purple, I can't do that with the current mechanics. Unless...
What if I need to think about this differently? What if the unframed block when split into purple-top + white-bottom is actually the CORRECT state, and the goal is just to have the framed block be white?
So the goal might be:
- Framed block: white (matching the top color of target)
- Unframed block: split into purple-top + white-bottom (matching the two-color pattern)
Currently:
- Framed: purple (wrong, needs white)
- Unframed: purple top + white bottom (correct)
So I just need to change the framed block to white! Let me click the white selector button.
But wait, I've tried this before. Let me check: when I click the white selector, the framed block becomes white and the indicator moves under white. Then if I press SPACE, the unframed block merges to all white. So I can't have both the framed block be white AND the unframed block be split.
Unless... SPACE doesn't need to be pressed at all. Maybe the goal is just to have the right configuration without pressing SPACE. Let me try just clicking the white selector and see if the level completes.
Actually, I realize I should check if the indicator position matters. The indicator is currently under purple. What if I need to move it to white? Let me just click the white selector and see if the level completes.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model updated: Now I can see the full layout. The indicator is under the purple button (cols 41-45). The target is white-above-purple. The unframed block when split is purple-above-white (reverse of target). 
Plan: Maybe the goal is simply to have the framed block be white (matching top of target) while the unframed block is split (showing two colors). Let me just click the white selector without pressing SPACE.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current state: framed=purple, unframed=split(purple top + white bottom), indicator under purple
# Hypothesis: goal is framed=white + unframed=split
# Try clicking white selector only

# Click white selector button (center around row 4, col 37)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 37}])
print("After clicking white selector:")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['pixels'] > 20 and node['color'] in ['W', 'p', 'g']:
        print(f"  id={node['id']} color={node['color']} pixels={node['pixels']} boundary={node['boundary']}")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['pixels'] == 5:
        print(f"  indicator: id={node['id']} color={node['color']} boundary={node['boundary']}")

print("\nDone:", last_action_result.get('done'))
print("Level completed:", last_action_result.get('level_completed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
